title: Baby-proof before the crawling starts
subtitle: They get mobile overnight — literally
short_summary: Crawling tends to arrive suddenly somewhere around six to ten months. The easiest time to baby-proof is before you need it.
wish_i_knew: You do not get a warning week — one day they are stationary, the next they are behind the TV.
why_it_matters: The big-ticket items are furniture anchoring (bookshelves and drawers tip), power points, blind cords, cleaning chemicals, medicines and stairs. A crawl around your own floor reveals more than any checklist.
what_to_do_now: Anchor tip-risk furniture, move chemicals and medicines up high, cover points in reach and check blind cords — then literally crawl the house at baby height.
what_can_wait: The full fortress. Doors, fireplace and pool-level things matter at walking; this round is about the floor world.
checklist_items: Anchor bookshelves, drawers and the TV
Chemicals and medicines up high or locked
Check blind and curtain cords
Crawl the house at baby height once
source_notes: Safety content — review against current Kidsafe guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A sturdy bookshelf with a friendly anchor strap and a small padlock badge, protective level-up feeling, navy and gum green accents.
image_alt: Pixel art anchored bookshelf with a strap and padlock badge, for baby-proofing.

title: Car seat sorted before the hospital bag
subtitle: The one thing you truly need on day one
short_summary: You need an approved rear-facing infant restraint fitted before you can drive your baby anywhere — including home from hospital.
wish_i_knew: The car seat is the only piece of gear you genuinely cannot leave hospital without.
why_it_matters: Australian law requires an approved child restraint from the very first drive. Fitting one properly takes longer than expected, and professional fitting services exist in most areas.
what_to_do_now: Buy or borrow an Australian-standard (AS/NZS 1754) rear-facing restraint and get it fitted — professionally if you can, or carefully using the manual and an anchor point.
what_can_wait: The pram, the nursery theme, the wardrobe. None of that is needed for the drive home.
checklist_items: Choose an AS/NZS 1754 approved rear-facing restraint
Book a professional fitting or fit it carefully yourself
Do a practice buckle with a teddy — genuinely helpful
source_notes: Safety content — review against current Kidsafe and state road authority guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A cute rear-facing baby capsule with a soft glow and small sparkle, sturdy and reassuring, teal and sunshine yellow accents.
image_alt: Pixel art rear-facing baby car seat with a gentle sparkle.

title: Finish the Centrelink claims
subtitle: The money side, wrapped up
short_summary: After the birth is registered, you can finalise Parental Leave Pay and check Family Tax Benefit eligibility. Both run through myGov.
wish_i_knew: The claims you started (or meant to start) while pregnant only finish after you confirm the birth — and Family Tax Benefit is a separate thing worth checking.
why_it_matters: Parental Leave Pay completes once the birth is confirmed. Family Tax Benefit is income-tested and separate; plenty of families are eligible without realising. Backdating rules exist but are kinder the sooner you claim.
what_to_do_now: Confirm the birth in your myGov claims, finalise Parental Leave Pay, and run the Family Tax Benefit eligibility check.
what_can_wait: Understanding every payment type in the system. Just these two are the core.
checklist_items: Confirm the birth in myGov
Finalise the Parental Leave Pay claim
Check Family Tax Benefit eligibility
source_notes: Government payment wording — review against current Services Australia guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A small piggy bank with a gum leaf sticker and a friendly completed checklist beside it, relieved and organised mood, cream and teal.
image_alt: Pixel art piggy bank and completed checklist representing finished payment claims.

title: Child Care Subsidy before the first day
subtitle: The CCS approval is the slow bit
short_summary: If childcare is on the horizon, the Child Care Subsidy claim through myGov should happen well before the first day — approval takes time and it is not automatic.
wish_i_knew: The CCS is not applied magically by the childcare centre — you claim it yourself, and unclaimed weeks can cost real money.
why_it_matters: The subsidy is income-tested and substantial for many families. You claim through Centrelink in myGov, then confirm enrolment details once the centre lodges theirs. Doing it early avoids paying full fees while approval catches up.
what_to_do_now: Start the CCS claim in myGov, and once your centre offers a place, confirm the enrolment when it appears in your account.
what_can_wait: Choosing final days and sessions — estimates are fine for the claim; details firm up with the centre later.
checklist_items: Start the Child Care Subsidy claim in myGov
Have income estimates ready
Confirm the enrolment notice when the centre lodges it
source_notes: Government payment wording — review against current Services Australia guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A tiny childcare backpack next to a friendly approved-stamp document, ready-for-the-first-day feeling, teal and coral accents.
image_alt: Pixel art childcare backpack beside an approved document, for the Child Care Subsidy.

title: Your baby book and free health checks
subtitle: The little book that runs the first year
short_summary: Every state gives you a child health record book (blue, red, green — colours vary) and a schedule of free child health nurse checks. They are genuinely useful.
wish_i_knew: The child health nurse visits are free, unhurried, and one of the few places you can ask every "is this normal?" question in one sitting.
why_it_matters: The book tracks growth, immunisations and milestones, and the nurse checks catch things early while reassuring you about the rest. Bring the book to every appointment — GPs and hospitals use it too.
what_to_do_now: Book the first child health nurse visit for your area and keep the book somewhere you can actually find it.
what_can_wait: Filling in every page. The health professionals fill the important parts; the baby-photo pages are optional joy.
checklist_items: Book the first child and family health nurse visit
Keep the health record book with the nappy bag
Write questions in your phone as they occur to you
source_notes: Check schedules vary by state — review wording before production use.
illustration_prompt: A cute health record book with a tiny growth chart sticker and a friendly stethoscope draped nearby, trusted and local, gum green accents.
image_alt: Pixel art child health record book with a growth chart and stethoscope.

title: Choose your pregnancy care pathway
subtitle: Public, private, midwife — all real options
short_summary: Australia has several pregnancy care options: public hospital clinics, midwifery group practice, GP shared care and private obstetricians. Some fill up early.
wish_i_knew: You choose a care pathway much earlier than you expect — and midwife programs can book out by week 12.
why_it_matters: Your care pathway shapes who you see, where you give birth and what it costs. Public care is excellent and free with Medicare; midwifery group practice offers continuity but has limited places; private care has out-of-pocket costs and needs obstetric cover on your health insurance well in advance.
what_to_do_now: Ask your GP about the pathways available at your local hospitals and put your name down for any continuity-of-care programs you like the sound of.
what_can_wait: You do not need to have a birth plan, a parenting philosophy or an opinion about water births yet.
checklist_items: Ask your GP which pathways your local hospitals offer
Check what your health insurance actually covers (if you have it)
Register interest in midwifery group practice early if it appeals
source_notes: Review pathway availability wording against current state health guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A friendly signpost with three gentle path markers leading toward a soft hospital, a small clinic and a home, warm cream background.
image_alt: Pixel art signpost with three paths leading to different maternity care options.

title: Crying peaks around week six
subtitle: It is a phase with a shape, not a verdict
short_summary: Infant crying typically increases from two weeks, peaks around six to eight weeks, then eases. It is developmental, it is not your fault, and there are safe ways through the hard moments.
wish_i_knew: The crying peak is a known, temporary pattern — and putting a safe baby down to take a breather is good parenting, not failure.
why_it_matters: Knowing the shape of the curve helps at 2am. If it ever feels like too much: put baby safely in the cot on their back, step away, breathe. Never shake a baby. If you are worried about the crying or about yourself, your GP, health nurse or PANDA (1300 726 306) are there.
what_to_do_now: Save the PANDA number, tell your support people about the six-week peak, and agree on a tag-out signal for the hard evenings.
what_can_wait: Fixing the crying. Some crying in this window has no fixable cause — comfort, safety and shifts are the strategy.
checklist_items: Know the curve: rises at 2 weeks, peaks 6–8, eases after
Safe cot + step away is always an option
Save PANDA: 1300 726 306
Agree tag-out shifts with your support people
source_notes: Sensitive coping content — review wording with clinical guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A gentle moon above a calm cot and an armchair with a warm cup of tea, quiet exhale mood, soft navy and jacaranda night palette.
image_alt: Pixel art moonlit cot and armchair with tea, representing calm during the crying peak.

title: The daycare sickness era
subtitle: Stock up before the first snot wave
short_summary: Starting daycare often means more bugs. It helps to have a basic illness setup before everyone is tired and covered in snot.
wish_i_knew: Daycare sickness is not one cold. It is a season of life.
why_it_matters: Having basics ready can make sick days less chaotic.
what_to_do_now: Prepare a simple sick-day kit and know your GP and after-hours options.
what_can_wait: You do not need to panic-buy every medicine. Keep it sensible and ask your pharmacist or GP what is appropriate.
checklist_items: Save your GP and after-hours details
Check what your childcare asks parents to do when children are sick
Ask your pharmacist or GP what basics suit your child
source_notes: Review illness wording before production use.
illustration_prompt: /card-images/pixel/px-daycare-sickness-era.png
image_url: Illustrated tissue box, thermometer and tiny socks.
image_alt: cute 8-bit pixel art item

title: Early screening: your call, no rush panic
subtitle: Options exist — and optional means optional
short_summary: First-trimester screening options (like combined screening and NIPT) have a timing window. Your GP or midwife can explain what each one does and costs.
wish_i_knew: Screening tests are optional, some have out-of-pocket costs, and the window comes around faster than expected.
why_it_matters: Some screening happens in a specific week range, so it helps to have the conversation early rather than discover the window has closed. There is no right answer — only what is right for you.
what_to_do_now: Ask your GP or midwife which screening options are available, what they cost and when they need to happen.
what_can_wait: Deciding instantly. You can take the information away and think about it — that is completely normal.
checklist_items: Ask about screening options at your next appointment
Check timing windows and any out-of-pocket costs
Take time to decide — optional means optional
source_notes: Medical content — review against current RANZCOG and state guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A gentle calendar page with a soft question-mark bubble and a friendly clipboard, no medical fear, teal and cream palette.
image_alt: Pixel art calendar and clipboard representing optional pregnancy screening choices.

title: Eighteen-month immunisations
subtitle: The easy-to-forget round
short_summary: The eighteen-month round of the National Immunisation Program is the one families most often drift past — the schedule has been quiet since the first birthday.
wish_i_knew: After the newborn flurry, eighteen months arrives with no fanfare — this is the round to consciously remember.
why_it_matters: This round includes boosters that keep earlier protection strong. Records stay linked to family payments, so staying current avoids surprise letters too.
what_to_do_now: Book the eighteen-month visit now while it is in front of you.
what_can_wait: Nothing else — this one is genuinely just "book the appointment".
checklist_items: Book the 18-month immunisation visit
Bring the child health record book
Note the next routine round is at 4 years
source_notes: Review against current National Immunisation Program schedule before production use.
illustration_prompt: A friendly string-around-the-finger reminder ribbon tied to a calendar with a bandage sticker, gentle memory-jog humour, teal and cream.
image_alt: Pixel art reminder ribbon tied around a calendar with a bandage sticker.

title: Feeding help exists — and it is free
subtitle: However you feed, support is a call away
short_summary: Feeding is rarely instinctive for anyone at the start. Free support exists: the ABA helpline, lactation consultants, child health nurses and your GP — for any feeding style.
wish_i_knew: Feeding help is not just for emergencies, and asking early is normal — not a sign something is wrong.
why_it_matters: Whether breastfeeding, formula feeding, pumping or mixing, the early weeks come with questions. The Australian Breastfeeding Association helpline (1800 686 268) runs 24/7, and child health nurses help with all feeding styles without judgement.
what_to_do_now: Save the ABA helpline number now, and raise any feeding questions at your child health nurse visit — none are too small.
what_can_wait: Having feeding sorted. Almost nobody does in the first weeks, whatever the photos suggest.
checklist_items: Save the ABA helpline: 1800 686 268
Ask feeding questions at nurse visits — all of them
Remember fed, safe and supported is the goal
source_notes: Feeding content — keep wording inclusive of all feeding methods. Review before production use.
illustration_prompt: A warm phone with a heart on screen next to a baby bottle and a soft cushion, supportive midnight-lamp mood, jacaranda and cream palette.
image_alt: Pixel art phone with a heart beside a baby bottle, representing feeding support.

title: One year: you actually did it
subtitle: The keeping-a-human-alive achievement
short_summary: Whatever this year looked like — smooth, chaotic, nothing like the plan — you kept a small human alive and growing for a whole year. That is the achievement.
wish_i_knew: The first birthday is mostly for the parents — bub will not remember it, but you will.
why_it_matters: A year of feeds, wake-ups, appointments, firsts and recalibrations deserves a pause. Keep the party as small as you like; the milestone is yours as much as theirs.
what_to_do_now: Mark it however suits you — a cake smash photo, a quiet beach walk, takeaway on the good plates. Take one photo of yourselves too, not just bub.
what_can_wait: A Pinterest party. A one-year-old is equally delighted by wrapping paper.
checklist_items: Plan something tiny (or nothing — also valid)
Take a photo with you in it
Look at the day-one photos and be a bit amazed
illustration_prompt: A single delightful birthday cake with one candle and a tiny gold crown beside it, warm celebration sparkles, coral and sun yellow palette.
image_alt: Pixel art birthday cake with one candle and a small gold crown.

title: First GP appointment
subtitle: The first boring-but-important step
short_summary: Book a GP appointment to confirm pregnancy, organise early bloods, discuss supplements and plan the next steps.
wish_i_knew: The first appointment is less dramatic than you imagine, but it starts the whole Australian pregnancy pathway.
why_it_matters: Your GP can help confirm pregnancy, organise early tests, discuss folate and iodine, talk through public/private care options and refer you into the right pathway.
what_to_do_now: Book a GP appointment and bring your estimated last menstrual period or positive pregnancy test date if known.
what_can_wait: You do not need to have every hospital or birth decision made before this appointment.
checklist_items: Book a GP appointment
Note your estimated last menstrual period if known
Write down any early questions
source_notes: Review against current Australian pregnancy guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: /card-images/pixel/px-first-gp-appointment.png
image_url: Illustrated calendar with a positive pregnancy test and coffee cup.
image_alt: cute 8-bit pixel art item

title: Four-month immunisations
subtitle: Round two — you know the drill now
short_summary: The four-month round of the National Immunisation Program is due. Same idea as two months — book ahead so the week does not get away from you.
wish_i_knew: The second round sneaks up faster than the first because now you are busier and more tired.
why_it_matters: Staying roughly on schedule keeps protection building as maternal antibodies fade. GPs, council clinics and some pharmacies run immunisation appointments; the visit is quick.
what_to_do_now: Book the four-month appointment now, and plan a quiet rest-of-day for bub (and you).
what_can_wait: Worrying about mild post-vaccine grizzles — a clingy 24 hours is common and okay. Call your provider if anything concerns you.
checklist_items: Book the 4-month immunisation visit
Bring the child health record book
Plan a gentle rest-of-day afterwards
source_notes: Review against current National Immunisation Program schedule before production use.
illustration_prompt: A friendly calendar with a small bandage sticker and a tiny gold star, second-time-around confidence, teal and sun yellow accents.
image_alt: Pixel art calendar with bandage sticker and gold star for the four-month immunisations.

title: The four-month sleep shake-up
subtitle: Not broken — upgrading
short_summary: Around four months, babies' sleep cycles mature and previously fine sleep can go sideways. It is a development leap, not a regression in the failure sense.
wish_i_knew: The "four-month sleep regression" is really baby sleep growing up — which is why none of the old tricks suddenly work.
why_it_matters: Baby sleep architecture permanently changes around now: more wakeful phases between cycles. It is common, temporary in intensity, and not caused by anything you did. Consistent, boring bedtime rhythms help more than any product.
what_to_do_now: Lower expectations for a few weeks, keep the safe sleep basics steady, and split nights with your support people if you can.
what_can_wait: Sleep training decisions, expensive sleep aids, or reading seventeen conflicting books at 3am.
checklist_items: Expect bumpier sleep for a few weeks around now
Keep bedtime rhythm boring and consistent
Split the nights where possible
Safe sleep rules stay the same
illustration_prompt: A sweet moon wearing a tiny level-up arrow, clouds and stars rearranging themselves, gentle humour, soft night navy palette.
image_alt: Pixel art moon with a level-up arrow among rearranging stars, for changing baby sleep.

title: Hospital bag and the flexible plan
subtitle: Packed-ish beats perfect
short_summary: A mostly-packed bag from around 34 weeks covers you. Babies keep their own schedules, and flexible plans hold up better than detailed ones.
wish_i_knew: The bag matters less than knowing how you will get to the hospital at 3am and who is watching the dog.
why_it_matters: Late pregnancy is easier when the logistics are boring and settled: transport, contact numbers, who to call, where the bags sit. Birth plans work best as preferences, not scripts.
what_to_do_now: Pack the essentials, write your preferences down loosely, and sort the 3am logistics: transport, parking, phone chargers, other kids or pets.
what_can_wait: Packing for every scenario. Hospitals have supplies, and someone can always bring the forgotten thing.
checklist_items: Pack the bag to 80% done
Sort transport and parking for any hour
Save the hospital and care provider numbers in both phones
Write preferences down — loosely
illustration_prompt: A cosy open overnight bag with tiny baby socks, a phone charger and a small toiletries pouch, warm lamplight mood, cream background.
image_alt: Pixel art overnight hospital bag with baby socks and a phone charger.

title: Introducing allergens: early and ongoing
subtitle: Peanut and egg in year one — on purpose
short_summary: Australian allergy guidance recommends introducing common allergens (including peanut and cooked egg) in the first year, once solids are going, and keeping them in the diet regularly.
wish_i_knew: The advice flipped a generation ago: introducing allergens early — not avoiding them — is what current Australian guidance recommends.
why_it_matters: ASCIA and the "Nip Allergies in the Bub" program recommend common allergens in age-appropriate forms (smooth peanut butter, well-cooked egg) around six months and not later than twelve, then regularly. Families with eczema or existing allergies should chat to their GP about the best approach first.
what_to_do_now: Read the Nip Allergies in the Bub introduction guide, and plan allergens one at a time, early in the day, in age-safe forms.
what_can_wait: Rushing every allergen at once. One at a time, a few days apart, is the calm way through the list.
checklist_items: Read the Nip Allergies in the Bub guide
One new allergen at a time, early in the day
Keep introduced allergens in the rotation
GP first if bub has eczema or known allergies
source_notes: Allergy content — review against current ASCIA guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A cheerful little bowl of smooth peanut butter and a soft-boiled egg with tiny sparkles, brave and positive food mood, coral and yellow accents.
image_alt: Pixel art bowl of peanut butter and a cooked egg with sparkles, for early allergen introduction.

title: Get Medicare and myGov talking
subtitle: Ten boring minutes that save hours later
short_summary: Almost every pregnancy and baby payment, rebate and record runs through Medicare and myGov. Linking them now makes every later step easier.
wish_i_knew: Half the paperwork of early parenthood is really just one login you set up while pregnant.
why_it_matters: Medicare rebates for appointments and scans, Centrelink payments and your future child's immunisation records all flow through myGov. Doing the linking while you can still concentrate is a gift to your future self.
what_to_do_now: Log in to myGov, link Medicare and Centrelink, and check your bank details are current with both.
what_can_wait: Any actual claims. This step is just plumbing — the payments come later.
checklist_items: Create or dust off your myGov account
Link Medicare and Centrelink services
Check your name, address and bank details are current
source_notes: Government service wording — review against current Services Australia guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A friendly folder with a gum leaf sticker, a small green-and-gold service card shape and a tiny padlock, organised and calm, warm cream background.
image_alt: Pixel art folder with a service card and padlock representing linked government accounts.

title: The 20-week scan
subtitle: The long look at how bub is growing
short_summary: The morphology (anatomy) scan usually happens between 18 and 21 weeks. It is longer than earlier scans and needs booking ahead in many areas.
wish_i_knew: The 20-week scan books out — organising it at week 16 beats scrambling at week 19.
why_it_matters: This scan checks how your baby is developing and where the placenta sits. Imaging clinics in busy areas can have waits, so booking early keeps the timing comfortable.
what_to_do_now: Book the scan for the window your care provider recommends, and ask whether you need a referral.
what_can_wait: You do not need to decide whether to find out the sex until you are in the room.
checklist_items: Get the referral from your GP, midwife or clinic
Book the scan for the recommended window
Decide (or happily not decide) about finding out the sex
source_notes: Medical content — review against current guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A cosy ultrasound picture frame with a tiny star and soft sparkles, warm and exciting, not clinical, cream background.
image_alt: Pixel art framed ultrasound picture with a small star, warm and friendly.

title: Parental Leave Pay groundwork
subtitle: You can claim before bub arrives
short_summary: Government Parental Leave Pay claims can be lodged up to three months before your baby is due. Employer leave also usually needs formal notice.
wish_i_knew: You can start the Parental Leave Pay claim while pregnant — and your employer usually needs about ten weeks' written notice.
why_it_matters: Doing the claim early means the payment side is mostly sorted before the sleep deprivation starts. Eligibility rules involve work history and income, so it is worth checking rather than assuming either way.
what_to_do_now: Check your eligibility on the Services Australia site, start the claim in myGov, and give your employer written notice of your leave dates.
what_can_wait: Finalising the claim. It completes after birth when you confirm the details — starting it is the win.
checklist_items: Check Parental Leave Pay eligibility
Start the claim in myGov (up to 3 months early)
Give your employer written notice of leave dates
Ask your employer about their own paid leave scheme
source_notes: Government payment wording — review against current Services Australia guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A friendly envelope with a small coin, a calendar sticker and tiny baby socks beside it, organised and hopeful, cream and navy palette.
image_alt: Pixel art envelope with a coin and calendar representing parental leave payments.

title: A quiet week — on purpose
subtitle: Nothing is due. Nothing is missed.
short_summary: No appointments, no forms, no milestones due this week. That is not a gap in the app — it is the actual state of things.
wish_i_knew: Some weeks the best parenting move is a walk, a coffee and absolutely no admin.
why_it_matters: The timeline only surfaces things when they matter. A quiet week means you are on top of it — enjoy the slack in the rope.
what_to_do_now: Nothing. Genuinely. Maybe the beach.
what_can_wait: Everything. That is the whole point this week.
illustration_prompt: A hammock strung between two gum trees with a coffee cup resting nearby and soft ocean light, deeply calm holiday-morning mood, cream and aqua.
image_alt: Pixel art hammock between gum trees with a coffee cup, for a quiet week.

title: Count the invisible wins
subtitle: You did a lot this week. It just doesn't look like it.
short_summary: Feeds happened. Naps happened (some of them). A small human was kept warm, fed and loved for another week. None of it goes on a CV, all of it counts.
wish_i_knew: Parenting productivity is invisible — the absence of disasters is the achievement.
why_it_matters: On the hard days it helps to name what actually got done: the appointments made, the tears soothed, the six thousand small decisions. Quiet competence is still competence.
what_to_do_now: Name three things that went fine this week. Bar is low on purpose. "Everyone got fed" absolutely counts.
what_can_wait: Comparing your week to anyone's highlight reel.
checklist_items: Name three fine things from this week
Tell your support person one of theirs too
illustration_prompt: Three small glowing trophy cups made of everyday items — a bottle, a sock, a coffee cup — on a warm shelf, gentle pride mood, sun yellow and jacaranda.
image_alt: Pixel art trophies shaped like a bottle, sock and coffee cup on a shelf.

title: Take the photo — get in the photo
subtitle: Future you wants proof you were there
short_summary: A quiet week is a good week to take the unremarkable photos: the messy kitchen, the pram in the hallway, you actually in the frame.
wish_i_knew: The photos you treasure later are the boring ones — and the parent behind the camera is missing from most albums.
why_it_matters: Milestone photos take care of themselves. The texture of ordinary weeks — breakfast chaos, the nap-trap couch, your tired happy face — is what disappears unrecorded.
what_to_do_now: Hand someone your phone today, or prop it up and use the timer. One photo with you in it.
what_can_wait: Sorting, printing, or making albums. Just capture; curate someday.
checklist_items: One photo with you in it this week
One photo of something gloriously ordinary
illustration_prompt: A cheerful instant camera printing a warm little photo of a family kitchen scene, tiny hearts floating, sentimental but playful, coral and cream.
image_alt: Pixel art instant camera printing a photo with small hearts.

title: Register the birth, add bub to Medicare
subtitle: The paperwork that unlocks everything else
short_summary: Registering the birth with your state registry and adding your baby to Medicare unlocks almost everything else: payments, immunisations, doctor visits.
wish_i_knew: Nothing else in the system works until the birth is registered and bub is on Medicare — and the hospital paperwork is only step one.
why_it_matters: Birth registration is free and done through your state registry (the hospital gives you the forms or a link). Medicare enrolment usually rides along with it or happens via myGov. Payments and immunisation records need both.
what_to_do_now: Do the birth registration as soon as the fog allows, then confirm your baby appears on your Medicare card in myGov.
what_can_wait: The birth certificate order can wait a little — but registration itself should not.
checklist_items: Complete birth registration with your state registry
Add bub to Medicare (often part of the same flow)
Check your Medicare card shows the new name in myGov
source_notes: Government process wording — review against current state registry and Services Australia guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A friendly certificate scroll with a tiny footprint stamp and a green service card shape beside it, official but warm, cream background.
image_alt: Pixel art birth certificate scroll with a tiny footprint and Medicare-style card.

title: Set up safe sleep before bub arrives
subtitle: Simple rules, calm nights
short_summary: Safe sleep in Australia follows Red Nose guidance: baby on their back, in their own safe space, flat firm mattress, no loose bedding, face uncovered, smoke-free.
wish_i_knew: Safe sleep is a short list of rules, not a catalogue of products — and most sleep gadgets are not on the list.
why_it_matters: Setting up the bassinet or cot to Red Nose guidelines before the birth means 3am decisions are already made. Many heavily-marketed sleep products do not meet safe sleep guidance.
what_to_do_now: Set up a safe sleep space to Australian standards: firm flat mattress, fitted sheet, nothing loose in the cot, in your room for the first six months if possible.
what_can_wait: Buying sleep aids, positioners, cot bumpers or wedges — safe sleep guidance says skip them.
checklist_items: Set up a cot or bassinet meeting Australian standards
Firm flat mattress, fitted sheet, nothing else in the bed
Read the Red Nose safe sleep basics once now
source_notes: Safety content — review against current Red Nose guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A calm cot with a firm flat mattress and fitted sheet under gentle moonlight, a small moon and star above, peaceful and uncluttered.
image_alt: Pixel art cot with a flat mattress under a gentle moon, representing safe sleep.

title: Six-month immunisations
subtitle: Round three of the early set
short_summary: The six-month round of the National Immunisation Program is due — the last of the tight early series before a breather until twelve months.
wish_i_knew: After six months there is a gap in the schedule — this round closes out the intensive early set.
why_it_matters: The six-month vaccines complete several primary courses. Timeliness keeps protection strong through the crawling-and-licking-everything era that is about to begin.
what_to_do_now: Book the six-month visit, bring the health record book, and enjoy that the next round is not until the first birthday.
what_can_wait: Nothing here needs to be perfect — a week or two either side is normal life. Just book it.
checklist_items: Book the 6-month immunisation visit
Bring the child health record book
Note: next round is at 12 months
source_notes: Review against current National Immunisation Program schedule before production use.
illustration_prompt: A calendar with three friendly bandage stickers in a row and a small trophy, completing-the-set feeling, teal and cream.
image_alt: Pixel art calendar with three bandage stickers and a trophy for the six-month round.

title: Start thinking about childcare
subtitle: Annoyingly early, but worth it
short_summary: If you might need childcare, start researching and joining waitlists earlier than feels reasonable.
wish_i_knew: Childcare planning starts before you feel ready to think about childcare.
why_it_matters: Popular centres can have long waitlists, and it takes time to compare locations, fees, availability, orientation and Child Care Subsidy.
what_to_do_now: Make a shortlist of centres near home and work, ask about nursery availability, and join waitlists if needed.
what_can_wait: You do not need your exact return-to-work date locked in before you start researching.
checklist_items: List centres near home and work
Ask about nursery availability
Check waitlist fees
Read the Child Care Subsidy basics
source_notes: Review Child Care Subsidy wording before production use.
illustration_prompt: /card-images/pixel/px-start-thinking-about-childcare.png
image_url: Illustrated childcare planning clipboard with blocks, dummy and calendar.
image_alt: cute 8-bit pixel art item

title: Starting solids
subtitle: Cute mess, but also a bit of a project
short_summary: Solids are about more than purees. You will be thinking about readiness, iron, allergens, textures, mess and routine.
wish_i_knew: Starting solids is less about fancy recipes and more about repetition, safety and not losing your mind cleaning the floor.
why_it_matters: This stage introduces new skills, flavours and routines. It can feel overwhelming, but you can keep it simple.
what_to_do_now: Get a high chair, a few bibs, some easy first foods and learn the basics of choking versus gagging.
what_can_wait: You do not need elaborate recipes or a freezer full of tiny gourmet cubes.
checklist_items: Check readiness signs with trusted guidance
Set up a high chair
Get a few easy-clean bibs
Read choking and allergy basics
source_notes: Review feeding, allergy and safety claims before production use.
illustration_prompt: /card-images/pixel/px-starting-solids.png
image_url: Illustrated messy high chair with pumpkin, spoon and bib.
image_alt: cute 8-bit pixel art item

title: Sun safety for little skin
subtitle: Slip, slop, slap — the toddler edition
short_summary: Toddlers outside means real sun exposure. Australian UV is serious: hats, shade, clothing and toddler-appropriate sunscreen become everyday kit.
wish_i_knew: UV here damages skin even on mild, cloudy days — the UV index, not the temperature, is the thing to check.
why_it_matters: SunSmart guidance: sun protection whenever UV is 3 or above — broad-brim hat, covering clothes, shade, and sunscreen on what is left. Building the hat habit at this age pays off for years.
what_to_do_now: Stock the kit (hat that stays on, toddler sunscreen, shade options for the pram) and check the UV index in your weather app.
what_can_wait: Perfect compliance. A toddler who mostly wears the hat is a win worth celebrating.
checklist_items: Broad-brim hat that actually stays on
Toddler-appropriate SPF 50+ sunscreen
Check the UV index, not the temperature
Shade for pram and play
source_notes: Safety content — review against current SunSmart guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A cheerful tiny sun hat with sunscreen bottle and a smiling sun peeking over a beach umbrella, bright Aussie summer mood, sun yellow and aqua.
image_alt: Pixel art tiny sun hat, sunscreen and beach umbrella under a friendly sun.

title: Words on the way: talking and hearing
subtitle: Wide range of normal, easy to check
short_summary: Language between 18 and 24 months varies enormously. If words seem slow, a free hearing check and a chat with your child health nurse are easy, pressure-free first steps.
wish_i_knew: Checking hearing early is the step that matters — lots of "late talkers" just had fluid in their ears all winter.
why_it_matters: Most toddlers say more words month by month, but ear infections and glue ear can quietly muffle a learning-to-listen brain. Hearing tests are free and gentle, and early speech support (if ever needed) works best early. Checking is caring, not catastrophising.
what_to_do_now: Mention any speech questions at the eighteen-month-ish nurse check, and ask for a hearing test referral if words seem slow or ears have been snotty all year.
what_can_wait: Comparing with other toddlers at playgroup. The range of normal is enormous — checks beat comparisons.
checklist_items: Raise speech questions at the nurse check
Ask about a free hearing test if unsure
Narrate your day — cheap, effective, dorky
Books at bedtime count double
source_notes: Development content — review against current guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A cute speech bubble sprouting like a small plant next to a friendly ear with a tiny headphone, growing-words mood, gum green and sky blue.
image_alt: Pixel art speech bubble sprouting like a plant beside a friendly ear.

title: Time for proper spoons
subtitle: Soft-tip baby spoons had a good run
time_critical: Your baby may be ready for more normal toddler spoons as they eat thicker food and practise self-feeding.
short_summary: Soft-tip baby spoons are great early on, but eventually they can make real food and self-feeding more annoying than helpful.
wish_i_knew: Around this stage, babies are often eating thicker textures, grabbing the spoon, chewing on utensils and trying to feed themselves. A slightly more normal toddler spoon can make meals easier.
why_it_matters: Buy 2-4 toddler spoons that are small enough for their mouth but sturdier than soft-tip baby spoons.
what_to_do_now: You do not need a fancy full cutlery set. A few good spoons are enough.
what_can_wait: Check if your current spoons are too shallow or floppy
Buy a small set of toddler spoons
Keep old soft spoons if still useful
Let baby practise holding one spoon while you use another
feeding_sensitivity: A cute illustrated toddler spoon presented like a tiny level-up item.
illustration_prompt: Illustrated toddler spoon presented like a tiny level-up item.
image_alt: approved

title: Tantrums are development, not defiance
subtitle: Big feelings, tiny prefrontal cortex
short_summary: Somewhere in the second year, big feelings arrive before the brain hardware to manage them. Tantrums are a normal developmental stage — including the supermarket ones.
wish_i_knew: A tantrum is a nervous system overflowing, not a child manipulating you — and not a reflection of your parenting.
why_it_matters: Toddlers feel everything at full volume with almost no self-regulation circuitry yet. Staying calm and nearby (not fixing, not punishing the feeling) is the evidence-aligned play. Public tantrums feel mortifying; every parent watching has been there.
what_to_do_now: Pick your calm script now ("I'm here, you're safe, big feeling") and use it on repeat. Guard sleep and snacks — most epic tantrums are half tiredness or hunger.
what_can_wait: Explaining logic mid-tantrum. Words come back online after the storm, not during it.
checklist_items: Have one calm script and reuse it
Protect naps and snacks — tantrum insurance
Stay nearby; connection beats correction mid-storm
Debrief later, briefly, when calm
illustration_prompt: A tiny storm cloud with a rainbow starting to peek through, and a warm cup of tea waiting beside it, weathering-the-storm humour, jacaranda and coral.
image_alt: Pixel art small storm cloud with emerging rainbow and a cup of tea.

title: Twelve-month immunisations
subtitle: The birthday-season booster round
short_summary: The twelve-month round of the National Immunisation Program is due around the first birthday — easy to lose in the party planning.
wish_i_knew: The first-birthday season is exactly when this round slips through the cracks — book it before the cake.
why_it_matters: The twelve-month vaccines include measles, mumps and rubella protection. Immunisation records also feed into Child Care Subsidy eligibility, so staying current keeps the admin side happy too.
what_to_do_now: Book the twelve-month visit near (before or after) the birthday, and bring the health record book as always.
what_can_wait: Coordinating it perfectly with the birthday — any date in the window works.
checklist_items: Book the 12-month immunisation visit
Bring the child health record book
Check your records updated in myGov afterwards
source_notes: Review against current National Immunisation Program schedule before production use.
illustration_prompt: A calendar with a bandage sticker wearing a tiny party hat, birthday-season-but-organised feeling, sun yellow and coral accents.
image_alt: Pixel art calendar with a party-hat bandage sticker for the twelve-month immunisations.

title: Two-month immunisations
subtitle: Book it before the week gets away from you
short_summary: The first major post-birth immunisation appointment comes around quickly.
wish_i_knew: Two months sounds far away when they are born, then suddenly it is next week.
why_it_matters: The Australian immunisation schedule has key vaccines due in infancy. Booking ahead helps avoid scrambling.
what_to_do_now: Check your child's due date for immunisations and book with your GP or provider.
what_can_wait: You do not need to understand every vaccine in detail before booking. Your provider can talk you through it.
checklist_items: Check the immunisation schedule
Book with your GP or provider
Add the appointment to your calendar
source_notes: Review against current National Immunisation Program schedule before production use.

title: Water safety: the quiet rule
subtitle: Arm's reach, always — everything else is bonus
short_summary: Babies and toddlers can drown quickly and silently in very little water — baths, pools, buckets, ponds, dams. Active adult supervision within arm's reach is the core rule.
wish_i_knew: Drowning is silent — the splashing-and-shouting version is a movie invention.
why_it_matters: Royal Life Saving guidance: within arm's reach in and around any water, empty the bath straight away, secure pool fences and gates (and never prop them), and learn infant CPR — courses run everywhere and take an evening.
what_to_do_now: Check any pool fence and gate actually latches, book an infant CPR course, and make "arm's reach, always" the house rule for every carer.
what_can_wait: Swimming lessons are lovely but they are a bonus, not a substitute for supervision at this age.
checklist_items: Arm's reach, always, near any water
Empty baths and buckets immediately
Check pool fence and gate latch — never prop the gate
Book an infant CPR course
source_notes: Safety content — review against current Royal Life Saving guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A calm ocean-pool scene with a bright life ring, a secure gate latch and a small watchful star above, protective coastal mood, ocean teal palette.
image_alt: Pixel art life ring and pool gate latch by an ocean pool, for water safety.

title: Whooping cough booster in pregnancy
subtitle: One jab that protects bub before day one
short_summary: A whooping cough (pertussis) booster is recommended in every pregnancy, usually between 20 and 32 weeks. It is free under the National Immunisation Program.
wish_i_knew: The booster you get while pregnant is what protects your newborn in the gap before their own vaccines start.
why_it_matters: Newborns cannot be vaccinated against whooping cough until six weeks old. Your booster passes protection across before birth. Partners and close family may also be advised to update theirs.
what_to_do_now: Mention the booster at your next appointment and book it into the recommended window. Ask whether household adults should update theirs too.
what_can_wait: Perfect timing debates. Anywhere your provider recommends inside the window is a win.
checklist_items: Book the booster with your GP, midwife or pharmacy
Ask whether partners and grandparents need an update
Ask about the flu vaccine at the same visit
source_notes: Medical content — review against current National Immunisation Program guidance before production use.
illustration_prompt: A gentle shield with a small heart and a friendly bandage sticker, protective and warm, no needles, teal and coral accents.
image_alt: Pixel art shield with a heart and bandage sticker representing vaccination protection.
